title: Preparing and submitting a job to a HPC cluster
text:
0.1 Contents
This is a simple and a bit unconventional approach for using High-Performance Computing (HPC) clusters. It covers how to set up Python environments with Mamba and submit jobs through the PBS scheduler. The basic logic can be adapted to other schedulers like SLURM, but the specific information required will differ.
The chapters are tailored to be executed in VSCode and probably work also in Positron or Antigravity. If you are not using one of these IDEs, you can still follow the instructions by copying and pasting the code snippets into a terminal. The main idea is to show how to set up your HPC environment and submit jobs in a way that is reproducible and enforce documented workflows.
For authorative information on how to use the HPC cluster, please refer to the official documentation provided by your institution or HPC services provider. This guide is a starting point but you can do more complex tasks and optimizations once you are familiar with the basics.

title: Preparing and submitting a job to a HPC cluster
text:
VS Code Terminal Behavior — Disable automatic terminal popups in VS Code.
Create your Mamba environment — Request an interactive session and install software using Mamba.
PBS Job Submission — Configure and submit batch jobs to the HPC cluster using qsub.
MetaCentrum Remote Development — Run VS Code server on a compute node and connect from your personal computer.

title: 2  Disable automatic terminal creation in VS Code
text:
3 Goal
The goal is simple: your commands should always run in the terminal that is already open.
crumbs: 2  Disable automatic terminal creation in VS Code

title: 2  Disable automatic terminal creation in VS Code
text:
4.1 Open your settings JSON
Use Ctrl+Shift+P and run:

Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)

Then add (or merge) the following keys:
{
  "terminal.integrated.hideOnStartup": "always",
  "terminal.integrated.enablePersistentSessions": false,
  "terminal.integrated.persistentSessionReviveProcess": "never",
  "python.terminal.activateEnvironment": false,
  "debug.internalConsoleOptions": "neverOpen"
}
With these settings, VS Code is less likely to open new terminals by itself. You can still open new terminals by clicking Terminal> New Terminal on the menu.
crumbs: 2  Disable automatic terminal creation in VS Code

title: 3  Create your Mamba environment and install software
text:
4 Note
This tutorial assumes that you successfully disabled automatic terminal creation in VSCode. If VSCode is properly configured, you can just open a terminal manually and click on the run button in every code cell, and the code will be sent to your currently active terminal. If VSCode is not configured yet, you can copy and paste the code snippets in this tutorial into a terminal.


5 Goal
Create and manage a Mamba environment on the HPC cluster so your software is ready before submitting jobs.


6 Connect to the login node through SSH
Open a terminal on your personal computer and connect to the HPC cluster’s login node using SSH. For example:
ssh USERNAME@skirit.metacentrum.cz
Replace USERNAME with your actual username and skirit.metacentrum.cz with the appropriate login node for your cluster.


7 Request an interactive session
Run this command from a login node to request an interactive PBS session. After a short time, you will get a message that your interactive session started and your terminal will point to a compute node.
qsub -I \
  -N mamba_setup \
  -l select=1:ncpus=1:mem=16gb \
  -l walltime=02:00:00 \
  -j oe


8 Run environment setup inside the interactive session
After PBS allocates the node and you see the interactive shell prompt, run:
module add mambaforge
mamba create --prefix /storage/brno2/home/gortega/mamba/envs/geostats -c conda-forge "r-terra>=1.8"
The code above will install R as a “side effect” of installing version 1.8 or higher of the terra package. You can replace r-terra with any other package you want to install, and Mamba will automatically resolve and install the necessary dependencies. Alternatively, you could just create an empty environment running mamba create -n SOME_NAME and then install packages one by one or in groups as needed. The option -c conda-forge tells Mamba to look for packages in the conda-forge channel, which is a community-maintained repository of packages. You can specify other channels if you need software that is not available in conda-forge.


9 Activate the environment
Here we will activate the environment to check that it works.
mamba activate /storage/brno2/home/gortega/mamba/envs/geostats


10 Install more packages
You can install more packages in the same environment. For python , just replace the package name after conda-forge with the one you want to install. For R packages you have to write r-PACKAGE_NAME.
mamba install -c conda-forge geopandas r-sf
The most recommended way to install packages inside your environment is to use mamba install... because it will automatically resolve dependencies and propose a working configuration for all the packages in your environment. However, you can still install packages from other sources (e.g., using pip for Python or install.packages() for R) if you prefer, but be aware that this may lead to dependency conflicts or issues with reproducibility.
Example of installing an R package from GitHub using remotes:
Rscript -e "devtools::install_github('gortegasolis/geomattR', dependencies = FALSE, upgrade = 'never', force = TRUE)"


11 Deactivate the environment when you are done
mamba deactivate


12 Exit the interactive session
exit
crumbs: 3  Create your Mamba environment and install software

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
text:
5 Note
This tutorial assumes that you successfully disabled automatic terminal creation in VSCode and have already created a Mamba environment on the HPC cluster. If VSCode is properly configured, you can just open a terminal manually and click on the run button in every code cell, and the code will be sent to your currently active terminal. If VSCode is not configured yet, you can copy and paste the code snippets in this tutorial into a terminal. Probably you’ll need Ctrl+Shift+V to paste in the terminal.
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
section: 11.1 General rule-of-thumb strategy for requesting HPC resources
text:
11.1 General rule-of-thumb strategy for requesting HPC resources
Run a small test job in your computer or in an interactive session on the cluster with a subset of the data (1-5% the whole dataset) and modest resources (1–4 cores, 8–16 GB RAM, 10–30 minutes walltime).
Measure:

Elapsed time.
Max memory usage.
CPU utilisation (are all cores busy or mostly idle?).

If runtime scales roughly linearly with the size of your dataset, increase the required time accordingly and add ~10–20% safety margin (e.g., if the test used 10 minutes for 5% of the data, you might need 4 hours for the full dataset).
For memory, take the maximum memory usage observed during the test, increase with input size if memory grows with data, then add ~20–50% safety margin if you’re unsure (e.g., if the test used 1 GB for 5% of the data, you might need 24 GB for the full dataset).
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
section: 11.2 Settings to modify before launching your HPC job
text:
11.2 Settings to modify before launching your HPC job
Modify these settings according to the requirements of your job script. The quoted example values are just placeholders you should replace.
# Write a name for your job.
JOB_NAME="demo_job"

# Number of CPU cores requested for the job.
NCPUS=4

# Total memory requested for the job.
MEM="8gb"

# Maximum wall-clock runtime allowed for the job in hours:minutes:seconds.
WALLTIME="01:00:00"

# Name of the script that will contain the job commands.
SCRIPT_TO_RUN="run_job.sh"

# Name of the submission script passed to the HPC scheduler.
SCHEDULER_SCRIPT="submit_job.pbs"

# Name of the Mamba environment used by the job.
MAMBA_ENV="/storage/brno2/home/gortega/mamba/envs/geostats"

# Working directory where scripts are created and the job is launched.
WORK_DIR="/storage/brno2/home/gortega/HPC-VSCode-Mamba_SciSeminar"
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
section: 13.1 Give execution permission to your job script
text:
13.1 Give execution permission to your job script
chmod +x "$SCRIPT_TO_RUN"
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
text:
13.2 Write submission script
cat > "$SCHEDULER_SCRIPT" <<EOF
#!/bin/bash
#PBS -N $JOB_NAME
#PBS -l select=1:ncpus=$NCPUS:mem=$MEM
#PBS -l walltime=$WALLTIME
#PBS -j oe

cd "\$PBS_O_WORKDIR"
bash "$SCRIPT_TO_RUN"
EOF

chmod +x "$SCHEDULER_SCRIPT"
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler
text:
13.3 Submit job
‘qsub’ is the function that will pick your PBS submission script and send it to the HPC scheduler system. The scheduler will then manage the execution of your job based on the resources you requested and the current load on the cluster. After you run this command, you should see a message confirming that your job has been submitted, along with a job ID that you can use to track its progress.
qsub "$SCHEDULER_SCRIPT"
crumbs: 4  Submit your job using the scheduler

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
text:
6 Note
This tutorial assumes that you successfully disabled automatic terminal creation in VSCode and have already created a Mamba environment on the HPC cluster. If VSCode is properly configured, you can just open a terminal manually and click on the run button in every code cell, and the code will be sent to your currently active terminal. If VSCode is not configured yet, you can copy and paste the code snippets in this tutorial into a terminal. Probably you’ll need Ctrl+Shift+V to paste in the terminal.
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
text:
8.1 Prerequisites

VS Code extension: Remote - SSH (Microsoft)
Your MetaCentrum account and SSH access to a frontend (for example skirit.metacentrum.cz)
A free TCP port between 7000 and 30000 (example below uses 26759)
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
section: 8.2 1. Create a local SSH key for this workflow
text:
8.2 1. Create a local SSH key for this workflow
On your personal computer:
ssh-keygen -f ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode
This creates:

private key: ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode
public key: ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode.pub
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
text:
8.3 2. Configure ~/.ssh/config on your personal computer
Pick your preferred frontend and chosen port, then add entries similar to:
Host skirit.metacentrum.cz
  Port 22
  PubkeyAuthentication no

Host *.cerit-sc.cz *.fav.zcu.cz *.fzu.cz *.grid.cesnet.cz *.hw.elixir-czech.cz *.ibot.cas.cz *.ics.muni.cz *.metacentrum.cz *.meta.zcu.cz *.natur.cuni.cz *.nti.tul.cz *.ueb.cas.cz
  Port 26759
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode
This tells VS Code to use your selected port and key when connecting to compute nodes.
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
section: 8.4 3. Start an interactive job and launch SSH server on the compute node
text:
8.4 3. Start an interactive job and launch SSH server on the compute node
First request an interactive job from a frontend:
qsub -I -N vscode_dev -l select=1:ncpus=4:mem=16gb -l walltime=04:00:00
After the shell opens on the compute node, run:
mkdir -p "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh"
chmod 700 "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh"
rm -f "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/server_key" "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/server_key.pub"
ssh-keygen -f "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/server_key" -N "" -C "VSCode" -q -t ed25519

touch "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/authorized_keys"
chmod 600 "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/authorized_keys"
Now append your local public key (from ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode.pub) into authorized_keys on the compute node:
cat >> "$SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/authorized_keys"
# paste one line from ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode.pub, then press Ctrl+D
Choose/check port and start SSH daemon:
PORT=26759
netstat -tan | grep -q "0.0.0.0:$PORT " && { echo "Port $PORT is busy"; exit 1; }

/usr/sbin/sshd -D -p "$PORT" \
  -o "HostKey $SCRATCHDIR/.ssh/server_key" \
  -o "PermitRootLogin no" \
  -o "PasswordAuthentication no" \
  -o "ChallengeResponseAuthentication no" &

hostname
Keep this job running. The printed hostname is the compute node you will connect to from VS Code.
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
section: 8.5 4. Connect from VS Code to the compute node
text:
8.5 4. Connect from VS Code to the compute node
Use Ctrl+Shift+P:

Remote-SSH: Connect to Host...
Enter/select the compute-node hostname printed by hostname

Because of your SSH config, VS Code will use port 26759 and key ~/.ssh/metacentrum_vscode.
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code

title: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
section: 8.6 5. Validate the connection in VS Code terminal
text:
8.6 5. Validate the connection in VS Code terminal
hostname
whoami
pwd
You should see your compute-node hostname, your username, and your working directory.
crumbs: 5  Configure MetaCentrum remote development in VS Code
